course.title: Maturity Model Assessment Fundamentals
course.description: A practical introduction to maturity model thinking — what maturity models are, how to use them effectively, how to interpret your scores, and how to build a credible improvement roadmap. Designed for practitioners and leaders who are new to structured capability assessments.
course.summary: Learn how to use maturity models to benchmark capabilities and drive transformation.

modules.title: What Is a Maturity Model?
modules.description: Understand the core concept and why maturity models matter for digital transformation.

content.html:
Why Maturity Models?

Every organisation operates at some level of capability across the dimensions that matter to its mission. Maturity models give that level a name, a score, and — crucially — a direction.

At their core, maturity models answer three questions:

  1. Where are we today? An honest baseline across the capabilities that drive performance.
  2. Where should we be? A target state grounded in strategy, peers, and best practice.
  3. How do we get there? A prioritised roadmap of investments and changes.

Without a shared model, improvement conversations fragment into opinion. With one, they become structured, evidence-based, and actionable.

"You can't manage what you can't measure. You can't improve what you can't describe."

Maturity models provide the vocabulary and the yardstick that makes both possible.

From CMM to Modern Models

The Capability Maturity Model (CMM) was developed at Carnegie Mellon in the late 1980s to help the US Department of Defense evaluate software suppliers. It introduced the five-level progression that almost every subsequent model has inherited:

LevelNameCharacteristic
1InitialAd hoc, unpredictable
2ManagedReactive, project-level
3DefinedProactive, organisation-wide
4Quantitatively ManagedMeasured and controlled
5OptimisingContinuously improving

Modern frameworks — CMMI, ITIL, ISO, industry-specific models — all follow this pattern, adapted to their domain. The Orion platform hosts multiple models, each tuned to a specific capability area.

content.description: Simon Sinek explores how purpose-driven organisations inspire action and build lasting capability — a powerful lens for understanding why maturity improvement programmes succeed or fail.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the primary purpose of a maturity model?

options.text: To rank organisations against competitors for marketing purposes
To provide a structured way to assess, benchmark, and improve capabilities
To replace strategic planning with a fixed improvement sequence
To audit compliance with regulatory requirements

questions.explanation: Maturity models exist to give organisations a shared vocabulary and evidence-based baseline so improvement conversations can be structured and prioritised.

questions.text: At what level in the classic CMM model does an organisation move from reactive, project-level management to proactive, organisation-wide practices?

options.text: Level 1 — Initial
Level 2 — Managed
Level 3 — Defined
Level 4 — Quantitatively Managed

questions.explanation: Level 3 (Defined) is the pivotal transition where ad-hoc practices become institutionalised standards applicable across the entire organisation.

questions.text: Which THREE questions does a maturity model help answer? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Where are we today?
Who is responsible for the gap?
Where should we be?
How do we get there?

questions.explanation: Maturity models answer Where are we, Where should we be, and How do we get there. Assigning blame is not their purpose.

modules.title: Taking and Interpreting an Assessment
modules.description: Walk through a real assessment cycle and learn how to read your scores.

content.html:
Setting Yourself Up for a Meaningful Result

An assessment is only as good as the preparation behind it. Rushing through the questions without context produces a score that nobody trusts and nobody acts on.

1. Agree on scope

Are you assessing a team, a department, a product line, or the whole enterprise? Define the boundary before the first question is answered. Mixed scope = mixed signal.

2. Gather the right people

No single person has full visibility. Consider running the assessment with a small group of three to five stakeholders who collectively cover the domain — or run separate sessions and compare responses.

3. Assign a facilitator

Someone needs to keep the discussion moving, capture rationale behind the scores, and flag uncertainty. This person doesn't need domain expertise; they need process discipline.

4. Set aside adequate time

A typical Orion assessment takes 20–40 minutes when answered thoughtfully. Block an uninterrupted slot. Answers given in five-minute gaps between meetings are rarely reliable.

5. Be honest, not aspirational

Score based on current, consistent practice — not on what you plan to do next quarter or what happens occasionally in your best team. Inflated scores produce a misleading baseline and a wasted roadmap.

What the Numbers Mean

Orion presents results at two levels: an overall maturity score and per-dimension scores. Both matter, and they tell different stories.

Overall score

The overall score is a weighted aggregate across all dimensions. It positions you on the maturity scale (e.g. 0–100 or labelled levels). Use it to:

  • Track progress over time across repeat assessments
  • Compare against industry benchmarks (where available)
  • Communicate a single headline to senior stakeholders

Dimension scores

The dimension breakdown is where the real insight lives. A high overall score can mask a critical weakness in one dimension; a low overall score might not require equal effort across all areas.

Look for:

  • Your lowest dimensions — often the highest-impact improvement opportunities
  • Outliers in either direction — a dimension that is far ahead of the rest may indicate over-investment
  • Clusters — related dimensions that all score low often share a root cause

The improvement statements

Each answer option in Orion carries an improvement statement — a description of what good looks like at the next level. These are your primary input to the roadmap conversation.

lessons.title: Audio: Influence and the Change Conversation

content.description: Practitioner perspectives — listen to this excerpt from Dale Carnegie's classic on communication and influence, themes directly applicable to driving maturity improvement across teams and stakeholders. (~9 minutes)

slides.title: The Four-Phase Assessment Cycle
slides.html:
Effective use of maturity assessments follows a repeatable cycle rather than a one-time event.

  1. Baseline — take the initial assessment and establish your current state
  2. Prioritise — select the two or three dimensions with the highest strategic leverage
  3. Invest — run the initiatives, training, and process changes your roadmap calls for
  4. Re-assess — return to the model (typically 6–12 months later) to measure progress

slides.title: Cadence Recommendations
slides.html:
How often should you assess?

  • Annual — minimum for most organisations; aligns with planning cycles
  • Semi-annual — appropriate for fast-moving capability areas (e.g. AI readiness, cyber)
  • Quarterly — useful when actively running an improvement programme and tracking sprint-level progress

Avoid assessing more frequently than your organisation can actually act on the results — diminishing returns and survey fatigue set in quickly.

slides.title: Sharing Results
slides.html:
Who needs to see the results?

AudienceWhat they need
Executive teamHeadline score, strategic gap, investment ask
Domain ownersDimension breakdown, improvement statements, roadmap
PractitionersQuestion-level detail, benchmark comparison, specific actions

questions.text: When should you score a capability in a maturity assessment?

options.text: Based on the best example you can recall from the last five years
Based on current, consistent practice across the defined scope
Based on what you plan to have in place by end of year
Based on what your most capable team does

questions.explanation: Scores should reflect current, consistent practice. Aspirational or exceptional scores inflate the baseline and undermine the roadmap.

questions.text: Where is the most actionable insight usually found in an Orion results report?

options.text: The overall maturity score
The benchmark comparison chart
The per-dimension breakdown and improvement statements
The date the assessment was completed

questions.explanation: Dimension scores reveal where gaps exist, and the improvement statements describe what better looks like — together they drive the roadmap.

modules.title: Building Your Improvement Roadmap
modules.description: Turn assessment results into a credible, prioritised plan.

content.html:
Making the Results Count

An assessment result without a roadmap is an interesting document. An assessment result with a roadmap is a change programme.

Step 1 — Identify your critical few

List every dimension and its score. Then ask two questions for each:

  • How much does this dimension affect our strategic outcomes?
  • How far are we from where we need to be?

Plot them on a 2×2 (impact vs. gap). Focus first on high-impact, high-gap dimensions.

Step 2 — Read the improvement statements

For each prioritised dimension, read the improvement statement attached to your current answer. That statement describes the practices you need to build to reach the next level. It is your specification for the first initiative.

Step 3 — Write the initiative

For each priority dimension, define:

  • Outcome — what does 'done' look like?
  • Owner — one named individual accountable
  • Key activities — three to five concrete steps
  • Timeline — start date and target completion
  • Re-assessment trigger — the date you will return to the model to check progress

Step 4 — Get it into your planning system

A roadmap that lives only in a slide deck will not be executed. Move it into your existing project or programme management tooling so it competes for real resources alongside other priorities.

What Goes Wrong — and How to Avoid It

Pitfall 1: Trying to improve everything at once

Organisations that launch improvement initiatives across all dimensions simultaneously usually make progress on none. Pick two or three. Finish them. Re-assess. Repeat.

Pitfall 2: Letting the score become the goal

Once people know they are being measured, they optimise for the score. Build in qualitative checks — do the improvement statements actually describe your real-world practices? — to catch gaming early.

Pitfall 3: Assessing once and filing it away

A single assessment is a snapshot. Value comes from the trend. Schedule your next assessment before you leave the room from the first one.

Pitfall 4: No executive sponsor

Improvement requires resources. Resources require budget. Budget requires a sponsor who has read the results and committed to acting on them. If you don't have one, get one before you build the roadmap.

Pitfall 5: Skipping the 'why'

Teams that don't understand why they are being assessed often see it as surveillance or box-ticking. Invest five minutes before every assessment cycle to explain the purpose and show how previous results led to visible change.

questions.text: When prioritising which dimensions to improve first, you should focus on dimensions that are:

options.text: Easiest to improve quickly, regardless of strategic value
High in strategic impact and significant in gap from the target
Most visible to senior stakeholders
Already close to the target, to demonstrate quick wins

questions.explanation: Prioritisation should balance strategic impact and the size of the gap. High-impact, high-gap dimensions deliver the most value when improved.

questions.text: Which of the following is a key risk of trying to improve all dimensions simultaneously?

options.text: It makes the overall score go up too quickly
It spreads effort so thin that progress is made on none of them
It confuses the benchmark comparison
It requires executive sponsorship across every team

questions.explanation: Spreading effort across too many priorities simultaneously is one of the most common causes of improvement programme failure.

questions.text: What should you do before leaving the room after your first assessment session?

options.text: Delete the draft and wait for a better time to assess
Schedule the date of the next assessment
Share the results publicly before further analysis
Assign improvement actions to every dimension

questions.explanation: Scheduling the next assessment immediately ensures the trend data that gives maturity models their real value. Without it, assessment becomes a one-off event.

questions.text: An initiative in an improvement roadmap should include: (select all that apply)

options.text: A clear outcome description
A single named owner
A list of every employee affected
A re-assessment trigger date

questions.explanation: An effective initiative needs an outcome (what done looks like), an owner (accountability), and a re-assessment date (feedback loop). Listing every affected employee is not a standard requirement.

content.statement: I confirm that I have completed the Maturity Model Assessment Fundamentals course and understand how to take a maturity assessment, interpret results, and build an improvement roadmap. I commit to applying these principles when participating in or facilitating maturity assessments within my organisation.
